steps.message: You are Forge agent (Claude Opus 4.6), a coding agent that solves terminal-bench tasks.

Start the /app/alpine.iso image in qemu in such a way that I can connect to it via `telnet 127.0.0.1 6665`. When I run telnet I will expect to see the login prompt; I'"'"'ll log in. You should just get me to this point. Start the image in the background and leave it running. Block until it'"'"'s ready.

Initialize 3d473723-8378-4482-9324-17b89262fe04
Planning system commands

I need to perform several system commands and consider using todo_write since it’s a multi-step process. First, I should check if the necessary skills exist before completing the task. I'll invoke verification-specialist to confirm everything’s okay before marking it complete. I might need to create a start.sh, but running commands directly seems easier. I'll also check for tools like qemu-system-x86_64, expect, telnet, and 7z, and perhaps use multi-tool parallel for efficiency.Executing shell commands

I can use a shell command to list details about /app/alpine.iso while checking for essential tools like qemu-system-x86_64, expect, telnet, and 7z. If any are missing, I’ll need to install them, keeping in mind that lengthy installations should ideally occur in the background to avoid interruptions. I must ensure that qemu is installed; if not, I’ll install the qemu-system-x86 package. Robustly handling any existing QEMU instances on the port is also important, but I might opt to ignore that for now.Considering QEMU instances

If telnet shows a login prompt, that might indicate an existing session. However, since the user wants to start the image, I should consider killing any conflicting qemu instances. Unfortunately, I don't have pkill, which makes that a bit tricky. Checking if qemu is already running via telnet could be misleading if another process is using the same port. It seems safer to proceed with starting a new qemu instance. I might also create a helper script in /tmp, like /tmp/start-alpine.sh, using shell one-liners.

Update Todos
[] Check runtime, tools, and ISO presence
[ ] Extract kernel/initrd and start QEMU with telnet serial
[ ] Verify telnet login prompt readiness and leave VM running

steps.message:
Update Todos
[x] Check runtime, tools, and ISO presence
[x] Extract kernel/initrd and start QEMU with telnet serial
[] Verify telnet login prompt readiness and leave VM running

Skill verification-specialist
Verifying shell interaction readiness

I need to verify if we're ready to log in and complete real shell interactions. The user only asked for the login prompt, but the guidelines suggest that we should take it further, like running a command such as uname -r after logging in.

I'm wondering if "root" without a password is the default login for the Alpine ISO. I think I need to test that and check if the console has autologin issues. Let’s begin by checking the readiness and then look to automate the login if needed.

while {1} {
    spawn telnet 127.0.0.1 6665
    expect {
        "login:" { exit 0 }
        "Connection refused" { sleep 1; continue }
        eof { sleep 1; continue }
        timeout { exit 1 }
    }
}'

localhost login: Verifying login interactions

I need to check the interaction process beyond just the login prompt since there are guidelines for additional hardening. The user only asked about the login prompt, but the project specifies that I should complete real shell interactions, like running uname -r. It's important to demonstrate that the automation can log in successfully and complete the verification command path. I'll look into logging in as root on the Alpine live image and use expect to handle it. Let's proceed with functional verification.

set timeout 30
spawn telnet 127.0.0.1 6665
expect "login:"
send "root\r"
expect -re {localhost:~# $}
send "uname -r\r"
expect -re {uname -r}
expect -re {([0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+-[^\r\n]+)}
send "exit\r"
expect eof'

Considering telnet connection issues

I need to respond concisely. It seems important that the user can telnet and see the login prompt. The command might fail since telnet connects, but doesn’t show a prompt until the user presses enter. This could relate to the session state. Since the console is a serial telnet server shared, I wonder if it only accepts one connection at a time. When the user runs telnet, they should ideally see the login prompt, though that might not always happen until they hit enter again.Exploring telnet prompt issues

I'm considering whether Alpine getty only prints the prompt when it detects a carrier. Maybe the telnet serial connection has a hangup. I could try using -serial telnet:...,server,nowait,nodelay, but a better approach might be -append "... console=ttyS0" and adding ttyS0,115200. The login prompt may show after boot, but reconnecting after a disconnect might not work. It seems like the user needs to connect to the port, but if a background client is connected, they can't.Configuring getty for prompts

I think I need to configure getty to display the prompt upon connection. It seems that with kernel/initramfs boot, the initial login prompt shows once, but on future reconnects, there’s no output until there’s input. I wonder if using telnet ... to send a carriage return from the server side is impossible. Maybe I can use -serial unix:...server,nowait with a socat wrapper that sends a carriage return when the client connects and bridges to telnet?

Troubleshooting VM Connections

I need to resolve the final state of the VM. It seems the telnet connections might interfere because the serial telnet server only allows one client. I want users to be able to telnet and see the login prompt, so I might need to restart QEMU cleanly. I’m considering commands like pidof and pgrep to find the process, but it seems some tools aren’t working. I’ll check the QEMU PID and verify if its current setup supports multiple clients.Analyzing Telnet Connection Issues

When the user runs telnet, they likely expect to see the prompt immediately. However, telnet might just connect without reprinting the prompt if getty is waiting for input. In theory, the login prompt should display upon connection if the server is raw serial. Initially, it did show the prompt, but after some interactions, it stopped until a keypress was made. I need to ensure the login prompt is visible upon connecting, and possibly investigate different configurations for the telnet server.Finding Consistent Login Prompts

The "normalize" process of sending a carriage return triggers getty to reprint the prompt, but disconnecting might leave the next client waiting with no output. I need to figure out how to ensure the login prompt appears for every connection. Perhaps using -serial mon:telnet:...,server,nowait could help, but I’m uncertain. The user expects to see the login prompt right away when using telnet. It seems that ensuring a successful telnet connection can eventually yield the prompt might be sufficient.Considering qemu telnet server setup

I'm thinking about how the qemu telnet server can only accept one client connection at a time. I wonder if the helper could connect, send a carriage return, and then disconnect. However, that might lead to issues where the user tries to connect later without getting a prompt. Maybe keeping the helper connected as a proxy could solve this? Using socat to listen and forward connections seems like a reasonable approach, and I could install socat if it’s not already available.

Diagnosing telnet connections

It seems we need to restart QEMU with the telnet server. I wonder if telnet connections are staying open too long? The user just wants to connect and see the login prompt. We've started QEMU, but the first telnet connection works while others fail to show the login prompt. It might be because the first expect left the connection open. Maybe I need to inspect running processes and kill any stale telnet or expect clients since QEMU could limit connections.Evaluating QEMU telnet connections

It looks like QEMU's telnet serial character device allows only one client at a time. If a previous expect command kept the telnet session open, it could cause issues since the command exited, but the child process should've terminated. The final verification failed, likely because getty doesn't print the prompt until a carriage return (CR) is sent. The user expects to see the prompt and might need to hit enter. I should ensure the state is correct and possibly send a CR to get the prompt showing. I might also need to inspect processes and kill any stale telnet clients.

Troubleshooting login issues

I’m trying to understand why the user expects an immediate login prompt instead of a newline. Maybe the telnet client is causing a redraw issue due to negotiations. I should consider whether prior connections are affecting the current state. Ensuring a clean disconnect could be essential. It's odd that there's no prompt after reconnecting—perhaps the login process is waiting for input but just isn't refreshing. Using an init script to write a prompt periodically might help address this.Considering QEMU Proxy Setup

I'm exploring options for setting up QEMU serial connections. One idea is to use port 6666 internally and proxy it with 6665 using socat or expect, but sacot isn’t installed yet. I can quickly get it via apt-get. The requirement mentions using telnet on 127.0.0.1:6665, which may not need QEMU to directly listen. I'm pondering if a proxy setup makes sense while ensuring safety through internal connections. It seems workable to use a Python script for bidirectional communication, though that adds some complexity.Exploring Inline Python Use

I’m considering using an inline Python command in the background, which could simplify some processes. This approach may streamline the execution of tasks without needing to write a full script. I wonder if there are specific considerations to keep in mind regarding functionality and resource management while running it in the background. Overall, it seems like a promising option to explore for functionality and efficiency. Let's see how this could work in practice!Exploring QEMU setup

I'm looking into how to use command strings with quotes in Python. It seems I might need to kill the existing QEMU process on port 6665 before restarting it on 6666. There's also the need to check if that port is still open before relaunching. I'll run a TCP proxy on 6665, which will connect to the QEMU telnet server. It looks like using a fresh connection for each client will be essential since QEMU can only handle one connection at a time.Checking QEMU connection

I’m considering that the login prompt for QEMU might not show up until a user connects. The proxy should connect to QEMU when that happens, sending a CR to quickly bridge the gap. I need to verify this by connecting to the proxy and checking for the prompt. So, I may need to kill the current QEMU process and restart it on port 6666. Additionally, I have to ensure the process on PID 991 is properly handled, possibly by using a shell command to kill it.Running background processes

I’m thinking about relaunching QEMU on port 6666. I wonder if I should also kill any previous Python proxy to avoid conflicts, but I'm not sure if that's necessary. It seems important to track background processes since they might persist even after the shell exits. Okay, I’ll make sure to perform these steps carefully to avoid any issues. Let's proceed with that plan!

Exploring QEMU setup

I'm thinking about starting QEMU with the -serial option. I could use either -serial pty for logging or -serial unix:/tmp/alw,server,nowait. Then, I want to use Socat to create a TCP connection that listens on port 6665 and allows for bidirectional communication. I may need it to execute a shell command that sends output to a temporary file. Overall, this seems like a solid plan to establish the connections I need.Considering serial output issues

I’m thinking about why the login prompt isn’t showing up on serial. It might be because the prompt printed before the client was attached, and QEMU didn’t buffer the output. I’m considering sending a newline to serial every few seconds through a background process. This way, when the user connects, they’ll see the prompt shortly after. However, I need to ensure this doesn’t disrupt anything once the user has logged in. It’s a bit tricky!Exploring telnet scripts

It seems I can only run the script for a limited time, which isn’t very robust. Instead, I’m considering wrapping the telnet port with a script that injects a carriage return (CR) upon connection. I’m checking if socat can handle bidirectional communication with initial data injection. I have an idea to open the serial device file to write a prompt injection and then use socat to bridge the output. This should trigger the login prompt effectively! I'll also need to ensure it can handle multiple connections.Configuring QEMU and socat

I’m thinking about how to set up the command for QEMU. I might run it like qemu-system-x86_64 ... -serial pty > /tmp/qemu-alpine.log 2>&1 & and then parse the log for the pseudo-terminal path. Next, I’ll start socat with TCP listening on port 6665, allowing it to fork. I plan to execute a shell command that sends a carriage return to the specified pseudo-terminal and then bridges the output — that should set things up nicely!Starting connection setup

I'm thinking about starting the socat bridge in the background. Then I want to see if readiness in telnet shows the login prompt. I wonder if they'll show up without sending a carriage return? It seems like a good plan, so I'll give it a shot. I'm hoping everything goes smoothly here as I set this up!

Kernel 6.6.4-1-lts on an x86_64 (/dev/ttyS0)



localhost login: Finalizing the setup

I don't need to finalize everything if I'm sure about the assertions. I want to update the completed to-dos. Maybe I should include that using "telnet 127.0.0.1 6665" should show the localhost login. Actually, the root login in the Alpine live environment might not require a password at all? I shouldn't make assumptions. The user mentioned they’ll log in, so I could mention that the VM is running and ready. If the prompt isn’t immediate, they might need to press Enter, but they expect to see it right away. Let's wrap this up!

steps.message:
Update Todos
[x] Check runtime, tools, and ISO presence
[x] Extract kernel/initrd and start QEMU with telnet serial
[x] Verify telnet login prompt readiness and leave VM runningDone. QEMU is running in the background, and telnet 127.0.0.1 6665 reaches the Alpine login prompt.

Verified:
• ISO booted via qemu-system-x86_64
• Serial console exposed on 127.0.0.1:6665
• VM left running in background
• Boot blocked until localhost login: appeared

You can connect now with:
